# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a multi-service application container that runs a web server (port 80), API server (port 8080), database (port 5432), and Redis (port 6379). Expose all necessary ports so the services can communicate. This is for our all-in-one development container.

Prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image
FROM python:3.8-slim  # Adjust this to your needs, e.g., use the latest version of Ubuntu or another OS you want for base images (e.g.: alpine)

# Set environment variables and install dependencies needed by web servers such as Gunicorn/uWSGI etc... Here we are using Python 3-slim image to avoid unnecessary packages installation, but in real production scenario it'd be better if you use the official python:latest or a specific version.
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1   # Ensure that Python doesn’t write bytecode into your image and don’t buffer stdout/stderr in Docker containers by default (this is a good practice to prevent unnecessary disk I/O) 
ENV PATH /usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin   # Add more paths if needed. This will ensure that Python can find its own packages and modules when it's running in a Docker container, which is usually the case for most applications
RUN apt-get update && \ 
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl gnupg2 lsb-release ca-certificates software-properties-common wget unzip netcat iputils-ping vim nano git openssh-server pwgen python3.8-dev libpq-dev
RUN pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel && \  # Upgrade packages and create a new environment with the upgraded versions of Python, which is safer than installing them into an existing image that has already been built by someone else using `docker build` or similar commands (which can lead to unexpected behavior)  
    curl -sSL https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/pgdg-apt.asc | sudo apt-key add - && \  # Add PostgreSQL repository key and update the package lists so that they are up-to-date with all packages available in their repositories, which is safer than installing them into an existing image
    echo "deb http://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt $(lsb_release -cs) main" >> /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgdg.list && \  # Add PostgreSQL repository to the list of repositories and update package lists
    apt-get update --allow-insecure-repositories   # Allow insecure repo updates (this is a security risk, but we'll use this for our multi service setup)
RUN pip install psycopg2 redis gunicorn uwsgi  && \     # Install required packages. You can add more if needed based on your application requirements and dependencies  
    apt-get clean all --force-yes      # Remove the temporary files that were created in `apt` calls to speed up image building process by removing unnecessary data from cache (this is a good practice) 
RUN mkdir /app && \     # Create an app directory for your application. This will be mounted as volume inside Docker container at runtime, so it's safer than mounting them into the existing images  
    wget -qO- https://github.com/apache/incubator-uwsgi-nginx-docker/releases/download/v0.9.18/run.sh | bash && \     # Download and run uWSGI Nginx Docker image
    chmod +x /app/*.py  &#34;      // Make sure the scripts are executable  
WORKDIR /app       # Set working directory inside container to app folder, so that it's easier for us when we mount this volume at runtime in our docker run command and also make changes into application files from host machine (this is a good practice) 
COPY . /app      // Copy all the contents of current dir(.) on your Docker image. This will be mounted as volumes inside container to access these directories, so it's safer than mounting them in existing images  
EXPOSE 80       # Expose port 80 for web server (this is a good practice)   
# COPY redis_init /usr/local/bin     // Copy the Redis init script to your image and make it executable. This will be run when Docker container starts, so you can use this as an entrypoint or command in docker-compose file  
ENTRYPOINT ["uwsgi", "--ini=/app/configs/nginx_prod.ini"]  # Set the default uWSGI service to start at boot time (this is a good practice)
